abstract: This article examines the distinctive features of modern globalization through the lens of Global Value Chains (GVCs), specifically addressing the implications for countries in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE). We analyze the unique challenges these economies face—such as the risk of the "middle-income trap" and a reliance on low-cost labor—and explore how public policy can be reshaped to foster growth. A central focus is placed on the role of technological development as a vehicle for capturing higher value-added activities within international production networks. To move beyond theoretical discourse, we support our findings with empirical data, utilizing original indices that measure CEE economic integration into GVCs alongside a comprehensive review of current economic literature.
